verdicts.uncertainty_note: The primary source (1983 abstract) names "tetracycline" as the inducer, not the "anhydrotetracycline (aTc)" asserted in the claim; aTc as inducer is later/background knowledge, not in PMID 6311683. Full text of the 1983 Gene paper is not in PMC, so judgment rests on the abstract.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Expression of both tetA and tetR is induced by tetracycline. ... We propose that two similar regions of dyad symmetry within the Tn10 tet regulatory region are operator sites at which tet repressor binds to tet DNA, thereby inhibiting transcription initiation at the tetA and tetR promoters.
verdicts.reasoning: The asserted quote is flagged quote_source="catalog-doc" and matches the part's own description field verbatim; it is not a sentence from Bertrand et al. 1983, so it is self-referential. I retrieved the genuine PubMed metadata/abstract for PMID 6311683 (a PMC full-text lookup returned an unrelated timolol paper due to an ID collision, so I relied on the verified abstract). The abstract directly supports the TetR-repressible, inducible mechanism (tet repressor binds operators to inhibit the divergent tetA/tetR promoters; expression is induced). However, the abstract names the inducer as "tetracycline," whereas the claim specifies "anhydrotetracycline (aTc)," which this primary source does not state — so the claim is only partially supported. Keeping ai-generated because the verdict is not "confirmed."

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The cited paper (PMID 10452611) supports the solubility function and the GST/thioredoxin comparison, but does NOT support the claim's "intrinsic to MBP fold" mechanism — that wording is the title of a different paper (PMID 23166722), so the bundled mechanism sub-claim is over-attributed to this source; I could not reach the 1999 full-text Discussion to read its actual proposed model.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Remarkably, we found that MBP is a far more effective solubilizing agent than the other two fusion partners.
verdicts.reasoning: According to PubMed, the cited primary source (Kapust & Waugh 1999, Protein Sci, DOI 10.1110/ps.8.8.1668) has a title identical verbatim to the asserted quote, and its abstract directly supports the solubility-enhancement function and the comparison ("MBP is a far more effective solubilizing agent than the other two fusion partners" = GST and TRX). However, the claim_value bundles a mechanism field — "intrinsic to MBP structure; passenger folding may be spontaneous or require endogenous chaperones" — and the claim_label's "intrinsic property of the MBP fold" wording is taken from a DIFFERENT paper (Raran-Kurussi & Waugh 2012, PMID 23166722), which the catalog correctly cites under a separate claim (solubility_mechanism_intrinsic). The 1999 abstract instead frames the mechanism as MBP "functioning as a general molecular chaperone" via "a model is proposed," not as an established intrinsic-fold property. Thus the function+comparison are strongly supported by the cited primary source but the intrinsic-mechanism sub-claim is over-attributed to PMID 10452611; the full-text Discussion was not accessible to confirm the model details.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: I read the verbatim title plus the full abstract via PubMed metadata, not the PMC full text; the abstract independently substantiates both components, so this does not weaken the verdict.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Hence, MBP serves as a passive participant in the folding process; passenger proteins either fold spontaneously or with the assistance of chaperones.
verdicts.reasoning: The asserted quote is byte-for-byte identical to the article's title as returned by PubMed metadata (Raran-Kurussi & Waugh, PLoS One 2012, the originating primary source), so quote_check is verbatim_in_primary. The abstract independently supports both parts of the claim_value: intrinsic solubility enhancement ("we can reproduce the solubilizing activity of MBP in a simple in vitro system, indicating that no additional factors are required") and passenger folding ("passenger proteins either fold spontaneously or with the assistance of chaperones"). The PMID, DOI, and section ("title") all check out, and the cited source is the primary paper itself, not a review or the catalog doc.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The primary abstract/title I accessed confirms amylose binding and one-step affinity purification but does NOT explicitly state "elution with maltose" — that specific elution detail rests on the broader MBP system, not on the text I read.
verdicts.evidence_quote: The peptides are fused to the C terminus of maltose-binding protein (MBP), which allows them to be purified by the MBP's affinity to cross-linked amylose (starch).
verdicts.reasoning: I retrieved the primary source (di Guan et al., Gene 1988, PMID 2843437; DOI 10.1016/0378-1119(88)90004-2) metadata + abstract from PubMed. The asserted quote is the paper's exact title (verbatim). The abstract directly supports the core claim: MBP fusions are purified by MBP's affinity to cross-linked amylose resin, in a one-step expression/purification vector system — matching function=affinity_purification, matrix=amylose resin. The only sub-detail not explicitly in the title/abstract is maltose elution, which is the standard competitive-elution behavior of this system and corroborated by secondary sources but not the primary text I read; this is a minor gap so I keep confidence high for the central claim.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: Could not reach the 1984 JBC full text (403); judged on the PubMed abstract, which is a sequencing paper and does not report ligand binding, maltodextrins, or amylose affinity.
verdicts.evidence_quote: The sequences of the malE gene and of its mature product, the maltose-binding protein, have been determined and are in good agreement.
verdicts.reasoning: The asserted quote is verbatim, but it is only the paper's TITLE. I read the full PubMed abstract of PMID 6088507 (Duplay et al. 1984): it is a gene/protein-sequence determination paper covering mRNA stem-loops, a deletion variant, and sugar-binding-protein homologies. It never reports that the ligands are maltose AND maltodextrin, never gives binding/affinity, and never mentions amylose resin or affinity purification. The title names "maltose-binding protein" only as the protein's name, not maltodextrin as a ligand nor the amylose-handle mechanism. The claim's biology is in fact correct but is supported by UniProt P0AEX9 ("Binds maltose and higher maltodextrins such as maltotriose"; KM=0.9 uM maltose) and by the 1988 vector papers for the amylose-resin handle — not by the cited primary source. Hence the claim is true in substance but the cited primary source does not support it.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: Only the abstract (not full text) was accessed, but the abstract alone is fully dispositive; the asserted quote is the paper's title and is only weakly evocative of tag removal — the substantive support is in the abstract body.
verdicts.evidence_quote: The fusion protein contains the recognition sequence (Ile-Glu-Gly-Arg) for blood coagulation factor Xa protease between the two domains. Cleavage by factor Xa separates the two domains and the target protein domain can then be purified away from the MBP domain by repeating the affinity chromatography step.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the PubMed metadata/abstract for the asserted primary source PMID 3073105 (Maina et al., Gene 1988, DOI 10.1016/0378-1119(88)90170-9). The abstract explicitly states the fusion contains the Factor Xa recognition sequence (Ile-Glu-Gly-Arg) between the MBP and target domains and that cleavage by Factor Xa separates the two domains — directly confirming tag removal by site-specific Factor Xa cleavage at a site engineered into the inter-domain linker, not within MBP. The asserted quote is the paper's verbatim title; it is genuinely from the primary source but only weakly states "separation from," so I cite the abstract body as the load-bearing evidence. All elements of the claim (protease=Factor Xa, engineered linker site, vector-supplied recognition sequence) are supported.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The asserted quote_source was "catalog-doc" (a paraphrase), but the figure and strength-class are verbatim in the primary abstract; I read the abstract, not the full text.
verdicts.evidence_quote: A plasmid vector has been constructed that directs the synthesis of high levels (approximately 2% of total cellular protein) of fusions between a target protein and maltose-binding protein (MBP) in Escherichia coli.
verdicts.reasoning: I fetched PMID 3073105 (Maina et al., Gene 1988) metadata/abstract directly from PubMed. The primary abstract states the vector directs "high levels (approximately 2% of total cellular protein)" of MBP fusions — directly matching the claim's level="high" and approx_fraction_total_protein="~2%". The catalog's asserted quote is a paraphrase, but the substantive figure is verbatim in the primary source itself, so primary support is genuine despite the "catalog-doc"/"secondary" labeling. The figure refers to fusion protein accumulation, which is what the claim asserts.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The paper directly establishes E. coli K12 origin; the "primarily used in E. coli" usage portion is not addressed by this 1984 sequence paper, but it is the central host_range assertion (native origin) that is verified.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Sequences of the malE gene and of its product, the maltose-binding protein of Escherichia coli K12.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the PubMed metadata and abstract for PMID 6088507 (Duplay et al., J Biol Chem 1984; DOI 10.1016/S0021-9258(18)91005-7). The asserted quote "the maltose-binding protein of Escherichia coli K12" is a verbatim substring of the paper's title. The abstract confirms MBP is the malE gene product of E. coli K12 (396-aa pre-protein yielding 370-aa mature protein), and MeSH terms include "Escherichia coli" and "Species Specificity." This directly supports the claim's origin/native-host core (E. coli K12 malE gene product, host = E. coli). The "primarily used in" usage aspect is not addressed by this sequence paper, but the host/origin assertion that defines the claim is strongly and directly supported by the primary source.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: I accessed the title plus full abstract (not the full text); the claim's specifics — engineered surface-entropy MBP variant, FAK/PYK2 passengers, interlaced domain-swapped dimers, and the structural-characterization caution — are all explicit in the abstract, so the abstract is fully sufficient here.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Here we report that a commonly used engineered MBP version (mutated to lower its surface entropy) can form interlaced dimers when fused to short protein sequences derived from the focal adhesion kinase (FAK) or the homologous protein tyrosine kinase 2 (PYK2).
verdicts.reasoning: I retrieved the PubMed metadata for PMID 31892719, which returned the verbatim title (matching the asserted quote exactly) plus the full abstract. The abstract independently confirms every element of the claim: passenger-promoted interlaced (domain-swapped) dimerization, observed in a common engineered surface-entropy-reduced MBP variant, with an explicit caution that it "might mislead experimental characterization of the fused protein sequences." The asserted quote is the paper's own title and is primary. Source title/abstract confirms the claim; only the deeper crystallographic mechanism lives in the unread full text, but the claim as stated is fully covered.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The asserted quote ("a common variant of the maltose-binding protein") is verbatim from the title but is generic; the substantive parts of the claim (8 mutations, surface-entropy reduction, crystallization aid) are supported by OTHER verbatim sentences in the same primary paper, not by the cited quote itself.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Subsequently, this tendency to crystallize has been further increased by an MBP version engineered to reduce surface entropy(MBPeng).
verdicts.reasoning: I read the full text and abstract of the primary paper (PMID 31892719, PMC6938514) via PubMed/PMC. Every element of the claim is directly supported by the primary source: it names the variant "MBPeng" engineered "to reduce surface entropy" specifically to "increase the chances for obtaining well-diffracting crystals," states it is a "commonly used MBP form" with "36 structures of MBPeng deposited in the PDB," and the abstract gives the exact mutation count ("reverting two of the eight mutations introduced in the engineered MBP"), with individual substitutions listed (E173A, N174A, K363A, D364A, I369A). The asserted quote is verbatim in the paper's title, though the strongest support comes from other sentences. "Widely used" is a fair paraphrase of "commonly used." According to PubMed, DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-56718-y.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The cited primary abstract names only Ni2+ and Cu2+ (not Co2+ or Zn2+) and says nothing about imidazole/low-pH elution; those parts of the claim are unverified by this source, and I could only reach the abstract (no PMC full text exists).
verdicts.evidence_quote: Charged with Ni2+, it was evaluated for the ability to bind peptides and proteins containing neighbouring histidine residues.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the verified PubMed abstract for PMID 3443622 (full text not in PMC; Elsevier page paywalled). The asserted quote is verbatim the paper's title and the abstract confirms the IMAC mechanism: a nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA) adsorbent, comparison to the iminodiacetic acid (IDA) adsorbent, chelation of Ni2+/Cu2+, and binding of neighbouring histidine residues. However the abstract does not mention Co2+, Zn2+, or any elution method (imidazole or low pH), so the claim is broader than this single primary source attests. Core mechanism is strongly primary-supported; the metal-ion list and elution clause are not, hence partially_supported.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: Full text/publisher abstract were paywalled; the verbatim title was confirmed via Semantic Scholar metadata and the method via consistent secondary summaries, but "one-step" and "N- or C-terminal" specifics were not directly read from the primary text (the original method included a post-IMAC carboxypeptidase tag-removal step).
verdicts.evidence_quote: Genetic Approach to Facilitate Purification of Recombinant Proteins with a Novel Metal Chelate Adsorbent
verdicts.reasoning: I attempted full text (Nature, paywalled/403) and abstract (Semantic Scholar API returned the record but the abstract field was elided; scispace/ScienceOpen returned 403). I confirmed the asserted quote is the paper's own verbatim title via the Semantic Scholar Graph API for DOI 10.1038/nbt1188-1321, and multiple independent secondary summaries consistently describe the paper's core finding: a poly-histidine peptide genetically fused to a recombinant protein (DNA for adjacent His fused to mouse DHFR, expressed in E. coli) yielding hybrid proteins purified by immobilized-metal affinity chromatography on a novel metal chelate adsorbent. This directly supports the claim's mechanism (genetic His-fusion enables IMAC purification of the fusion product). I keep status ai-generated because I could only reach metadata, not full_text/abstract, and the "one-step" and "N-/C-terminal" qualifiers are mildly beyond what the title alone establishes — the original protocol included a downstream carboxypeptidase tag-removal step.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The asserted quote is the catalog's own paraphrase (quote_source=catalog-doc), and the specific "6-8 M urea/guanidine" numeric is not stated for His specifically in the His-tag section; but the substantive claims (native+denaturing, multi-host, small size) are verbatim in the primary review.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Purification using poly-histidine tags has been carried out successfully using a number of expression systems including bacteria (Chen and Hai 1994; Rank et al. 2001), yeast (Borsing et al. 1997; Kaslow and Shiloach 1994), mammalian cells (Janknecht et al. 1991; Janknecht and Nordheim 1992), and baculovirus-infected insect cells (Kuusinen et al. 1995; Schmidt et al. 1998).
verdicts.reasoning: The asserted source is a secondary review (Terpe 2003), and its catalog quote is a paraphrase, so quote_check is self_referential. However, I retrieved the actual full-text PDF of the primary review (via the MIT-hosted copy, cross-checked against PubMed metadata) and read the His-tag section directly. It states verbatim that His6-tagged proteins work "under denaturing conditions" AND can bind Ni2+-NTA "under native conditions," and that purification "has been carried out successfully using a number of expression systems including bacteria... yeast... mammalian cells... and baculovirus-infected insect cells," plus that the "relatively small size and charge" rarely affects activity. This is strong, direct support for both the broad host_range and native+denaturing conditions, so the claim is confirmed with high confidence.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The source supports the 2-to-10 length range but names neither "8xHis" nor "10xHis" explicitly (only the upper bound of ten), and "minimal" is inaccurate since the source's minimum is two histidines, not six.
verdicts.evidence_quote: The polyhistidine affinity tag, also known as the His-tag or His, usually consists of six consecutive histidine residues, but can vary in length from two to ten histidine residues.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the full PMC text (PMC4527311) of the cited review. Its Polyhistidine section states the tag "usually consists of six consecutive histidine residues, but can vary in length from two to ten histidine residues," which supports that 6xHis is the usual/canonical form and that length variants up to ten exist. However, the asserted quote (quote_source=catalog-doc about metal ions/NTA/IDA/placement) is the catalog's own paraphrase and is not the actual supporting sentence in the paper (self_referential), and it concerns mechanism, not variants. The claim's "minimal" wording is contradicted (source minimum is two, not six), and "8xHis"/"10xHis" are within the stated 2-10 range but not individually named (only ten is). Hence partially_supported rather than confirmed.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The asserted PMID 6526011 is WRONG — it resolves to Munro & Pelham 1984 (EMBO J, substance-P tagging), not the FLAG paper; only the DOI 10.1038/nbt1088-1204 correctly identifies the Hopp et al. 1988 primary source, which is not PubMed-indexed. The PMID should be corrected or removed.
verdicts.evidence_quote: A small hydrophilic peptide of eight amino acids (AspTyrLysAspAspAspAspLys) was engineered onto the N-terminus of a variety of recombinant lymphokines for the purpose of aiding in their detection and purification.
verdicts.reasoning: The asserted quote (the article title) is verbatim correct for the genuine primary paper at DOI 10.1038/nbt1088-1204 (Hopp et al., Bio/Technology 6:1204-1210, 1988), which I confirmed via the publisher page and a companion abstract. The paper's own content states the marker is "a small hydrophilic peptide of eight amino acids (AspTyrLysAspAspAspAspLys)... engineered onto the N-terminus... for detection and purification," directly supporting every field: function=epitope_affinity_tag, design=engineered_synthetic_peptide, length_aa=8 (matches part sequence DYKDDDDK), hydrophilic=true. The closely related Prickett/Amberg/Hopp 1989 abstract (PMID 2698650, read directly from PubMed) independently confirms "a short hydrophilic peptide marker segment." The one defect is metadata: PMID 6526011 is a mismatched/incorrect identifier pointing to an unrelated paper, but the claim and its quote are genuinely supported by the correct primary DOI.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: M1's "requires_free_N_terminus" and M2's "position_insensitive" sub-claims are NOT stated in the source's abstract (the only text reachable; no PMC full text exists), and the asserted quote is merely the paper's title, not substantive support.
verdicts.evidence_quote: One antibody, denoted as M1, binds the peptide in the presence of bivalent metal cations, preferably Ca(+). Elution is effected by chelating agents. Another strategy is competitive elution with excess of free FLAG peptide. Antibodies M2 and M5 are applied in this procedure.
verdicts.reasoning: According to PubMed, I read the abstract of Einhauer & Jungbauer 2001 (PMID 11694294, doi:10.1016/s0165-022x(01)00213-5); no PMCID exists so full text was unreachable (the full_text tool collided on PMC11694294, an unrelated ADC paper). The abstract directly confirms the existence of M1/M2/M5 anti-FLAG antibodies, M1 being Ca2+/bivalent-cation dependent, and M2/M5 used in peptide-competitive elution (consistent with Ca-independence). However, the asserted quote is just the paper's title (self-referential, not substantive support), and two structured sub-fields — M1 "requires_free_N_terminus" and M2 "position_insensitive" — are not stated anywhere in the abstract, so the claim is only partially supported by this primary source.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The abstract confirms FLAG includes an enterokinase-cleavage site; the finer sub-claims "cleaves_after K" and explicit N-terminal tag removal are standard enterokinase/DDDDK biochemistry not spelled out in the accessed abstract (full text was paywalled).
verdicts.evidence_quote: A fusion tag, called FLAG and consisting of eight amino acids (AspTyrLysAspAspAspAspLys) including an enterokinase-cleavage site, was specifically designed for immunoaffinity chromatography.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the PubMed abstract of the asserted primary source (Einhauer & Jungbauer 2001, PMID 11694294, doi:10.1016/s0165-022x(01)00213-5); the Elsevier full text was paywalled. The abstract states verbatim that the 8-aa FLAG peptide AspTyrLysAspAspAspAspLys "includ[es] an enterokinase-cleavage site," directly confirming the protease (enterokinase) and that the motif within DYKDDDDK is the cleavage site. The part sequence DYKDDDDK matches, and DDDDK is the canonical enterokinase recognition motif cleaving after the Lys; the asserted quote "enterokinase cleavage" is a faithful paraphrase of "enterokinase-cleavage site." The only items not explicitly in the abstract are the directionality and the words "tag removal," which are well-established consequences of this site rather than novel claims.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The structured contact/dispensable residues are exactly supported, but the catalog's "minimal_motif": "DYKxxD" string is a slight gloss — D4 is itself a contact residue, not an "x"; the paper frames its result as "a shorter yet equally binding version" (validates dropping C-terminal K8; D5/D7 dispensable) rather than literally stating a DYKxxD motif.
verdicts.evidence_quote: Five out of the eight FLAG peptide residues (Asp1, Tyr2, Lys3, Asp4 and Asp6) contribute directly to the interaction with M2.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the PubMed abstract (PMID 38852931) and the full-text content of the primary paper (Beugelink et al. 2024 JMB / its bioRxiv preprint, same work) via web search returning direct source paraphrase. The source states the five direct-contact residues are exactly Asp1, Tyr2, Lys3, Asp4, Asp6 (= claim's D1,Y2,K3,D4,D6), that Asp5 points away from the binding site and Asp7/Lys8 are unresolved (= claim's dispensable D5,D7), and that the FLAG peptide adopts a 3_10 helix — all matching the claim and the asserted (paraphrased) quote. PDB 8RMO is correctly the 1.16–1.17 Å Fab–FLAG(DYKDDDDK) co-crystal. The asserted quote is a faithful paraphrase, not a verbatim sentence, and the "DYKxxD" motif string is a minor imprecision, but the claim's substance is strongly and primarily supported.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The "Y2" residue numbering is derived (FLAG=DYKDDDDK has a single Tyr), not stated by the paper; and the effect was demonstrated mainly on a misfolded model GPI-AP — well-folded CD55-WT FLAG was unaffected, and one other monoclonal (Transgen) was not affected, so the caveat is context/antibody-specific rather than universal.
verdicts.evidence_quote: These results suggest that a FLAG tag on the misfolded protein would be sulfated, causing a reduced detection by the M2 anti-FLAG antibody. Attention should be required when quantifying the FLAG-tagged proteins in the secretory pathway.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the full PMC text (PMC8099120) of Guo, Gao & Fujita, PLoS ONE 2021 (doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0250805). The asserted quote is the paper's verbatim title. The text directly supports every claim component: a tyrosine on the FLAG-tag (DYKDDDDK has only Y2) is sulfated; SLC35B2 transports the PAPS sulfate donor and its KO raises anti-FLAG staining; "TPST2 plays a major role in transferring sulfate to the tyrosine residue"; the effect reduces M2 anti-FLAG detection and was also seen with the Proteintech polyclonal ("showed similar staining patterns with M2 antibody"); the context is a secreted/membrane GPI-AP and the authors caution about quantifying FLAG proteins "in the secretory pathway." Caveats: Y2 is inferred not numbered, and the effect is protein/antibody-context-specific (folded CD55-WT and a Transgen monoclonal were unaffected), but these do not contradict the claim as worded.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The claim is biologically correct, but the ASSERTED primary source (PMID 10956032) does not support it; the genuinely supporting paper (PMID 1902522, Dubendorff & Studier 1991) is in the reference list but is not the attached source for this claim.
verdicts.evidence_quote: We have examined the behavior of T7 RNA polymerase (RNAP) at a set of promoter variants having all possible single base pair (bp) substitutions. The polymerase exhibits an absolute requirement for initiation with a purine and a strong preference for initiation with GTP vs ATP.
verdicts.reasoning: I read the PubMed metadata/abstract for the asserted source PMID 10956032 (Imburgio, Rong, Ma, McAllister, Biochemistry 2000; doi:10.1021/bi000365w). It studies T7 RNAP promoter recognition and start-site selection using single-bp promoter variants (purine/GTP initiation preference, start-site selection, poly(G) slippage) and says nothing about the lac operator, LacI repression, IPTG induction, or the engineered T7lac promoter-operator construct. The asserted quote is verbatim the catalog's own description field (quote_source=catalog-doc), so it is self-referential and not primary support. The claim is in fact supported by a DIFFERENT reference in the part file, PMID 1902522 (Dubendorff & Studier 1991, J Mol Biol; doi:10.1016/0022-2836(91)90856-2), whose abstract explicitly describes LacI repression, induction with inducer, requirement for an inducible T7 RNAP gene, and very low basal expression — but that paper is not the source attached to this claim, so as asserted the source-claim link is disputed.

verdicts.uncertainty_note: The paper is not Open Access; only the abstract was machine-accessible, which confirms aTc as the inducer of a TetR/operator system but does not verbatim state the "TetR binding is relieved by aTc" de-repression mechanism, so the mode is supported only by inference from the abstract.
verdicts.evidence_quote: The novel promoter PLtetO-1 allows the regulation of gene expression over an up to 5000-fold range with anhydrotetracycline (aTc)
verdicts.reasoning: According to PubMed (Lutz & Bujard 1997, Nucleic Acids Res, DOI: 10.1093/nar/25.6.1203, PMID 9092630), I read the full abstract and the PMC page; the full body is behind a paywall (not Open Access, Europe PMC fullTextXML 404). The asserted quote has quote_source "catalog-doc" and is not a sentence from the paper, so it is self-referential, not primary support. The abstract does provide genuine primary support for the inducer identity: it names anhydrotetracycline (aTc) as the effector and frames the system around the "TetR/O" regulatory element / "Tet repressor," giving moderate support that expression is repressor-controlled and aTc-induced. The specific de-repression mechanism (TetR binds tetO; aTc relieves binding) is standard and consistent with the abstract but is not spelled out verbatim in the text I could access, so I downgrade from confirmed to partially_supported with medium confidence.
